text: Much of the literature on foreign policy in policy studies emphasizes the insignificant influence of Islam in the foreign policy of Indonesia. This article contends otherwise by referring to Indonesia’s diplomatic moves and actions undertaken during the struggle for Indonesian independence (1945-1949). Due to weak administrative conditions at that time, many societal forces carried out their foreign diplomatic activities more actively than the State Officials themselves. For instance, the student movements and Indonesian nationalist organizations had used the slogan of the ‘Islamic Brotherhood’ or ‘Ikhwan’ in order to bridge contacts with the Arab countries. Eventually they obtained the coveted diplomatic recognition for Indonesia through the mediation of the Arab League and some of the independent Arab states. International recognition of Indonesian struggle against the Dutch colonial rule on the world stage was thus in many ways made possible through the name of Islam. However, owing to the emergence of the Cold War rivalry, the unresolved crisis of Palestine in 1948, which sparked the new wave of anti-western activism in the Arab world and in other developing countries, Indonesia slowly began to change its foreign diplomatic tactics from an exclusive use of Islamic organisations and countries to more universally non-sectarian diplomatic activities particularly towards institutions such as the United Nations, hence terminating the functional aspect of Islam in Indonesian foreign policy on which ultimately contributed to the secular and pluralist diplomatic identity of Indonesia in the international political arena.
explanation: The text reads as a highly cohesive scholarly synthesis with broad, assertive claims and minimal citations, a pattern often seen in AI-generated prose. However, it could also be a plausible human-authored abstract or literature synthesis, so the conclusion is uncertain.
